title: Vomit on Carpet
timeEstimate: 30–90 minutes plus drying; pad involvement = pro
stainChemistry: Same vomit chemistry as on fabric (protein + stomach acid + bacteria) with added carpet-construction issues: pooled vomit soaks into the pad where bacteria can persist and re-emit odor for months. The biohazard posture + carpet extraction rules combine. Enzyme for protein, baking-soda / vinegar for acid neutralization and odor, hot-water extraction for bacteria — but home tools have limited reach into the pad. Pad involvement often means a pro extraction. Synthetic face yarn tolerates standard chemistry; wool narrows the lane.
whyThisWorks: Gloves + scrape + extract = reduce bulk before chemistry. Cold damp-cloth with enzyme pretreatment digests food protein. Baking-soda rinse neutralizes acid (protecting carpet dye). NSD handles residual bile-salt / food-oil. Weighted extraction between every pass pulls moisture up from the backing. Dry + vacuum finishes. Soapy or ammonia-based cleaners make it worse — ammonia smells like urine to pets AND leaves residue bacteria love.
defaultAssumption: Vomit on carpet is escalate-FIRST if: the carpet is wool, the spill soaked through to the pad (visible pooling > 1 second), the spill covers an area larger than a dinner plate, or odor persists after a full home cycle. Small spot, synthetic face yarn, caught fresh = reasonable DIY.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Disposable gloves, dull blade, disposable paper / bag
spottingProtocol.instruction: Gloves on. Scrape solid material off the carpet surface onto disposable paper. Seal in a bag. Don't push down — you'll drive material into the pile and pad.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD in cold water, damp cloth, weighted dry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold NSD via damp cloth — press, lift, rotate. Never pour. Extract with weighted dry towel between passes. Cold dilutes acid AND moves protein before heat can set it.

spottingProtocol.technique: Damp cloth enzyme for protein digest
spottingProtocol.equipment: Liquid Protein Spotter OR enzyme pretreat in warm water (30–38°C) on a damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dampen cloth with enzyme solution, press on the spot, dwell 15–20 min. protein-digesting enzyme digests the food-protein matrix. Keep the spot wet but don't pool.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 1 tbsp baking soda in 1 cup cool water, damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply via damp cloth. Neutralizes stomach acid — protects carpet dye from ongoing fade. Blot dry.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD damp cloth, heavy dry towel + weight
spottingProtocol.instruction: NSD-damp clean pass. Weighted dry towel 30 min to pull moisture up from backing.
Fan-dry. Once bone-dry, vacuum. Inspect next day for smell OR wicked-back ring. Either = pad contamination = pro call.

steps: 2. Cold NSD via damp cloth + weighted extract.
3. Enzyme pretreat damp cloth — 15–20 min digest protein.
6. Fan-dry + vacuum. Smell or wicking = escalate.
products: Liquid Protein Spotter or enzyme pretreat
warnings: Never pour or pool liquid — pad contamination
Never use hot water — sets protein AND activates backing adhesive
Never ammonia-based cleaners on any body-fluid stain on carpet — attracts pets
Wool carpet narrows chemistry — escalate earlier

homeSolutions.instruction: Vomit on carpet escalates faster than most stains. If the spill soaked into the pad (liquid pooled for more than a second), covers an area larger than a dinner plate, the carpet is wool, or this is a repeat event — call a carpet cleaner. Pad contamination is invisible and re-emits odor for months. A pro with hot-water extraction reaches depths you can't.

homeSolutions.agent: Gloves, Scrape Solids, Seal in Bag
homeSolutions.instruction: Put on gloves. Use a dull butter knife or plastic scraper to lift solid material off the carpet onto a paper towel. Seal the debris in a bag. Don't push down on the carpet — you'll drive material deeper.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water + Dawn on a DAMP Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: Dampen a white cloth with cool water + a drop of Dawn. Press onto the spot, lift, rotate. Never pour water on the carpet — pooled liquid ruins the pad. Keep the cloth damp, not dripping. 2–3 cycles.

homeSolutions.agent: Enzyme Pretreat on a Damp Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: Dissolve an enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz) in WARM water per the label. Dampen a cloth with that solution, press onto the spot, let sit 15–20 minutes. The enzymes digest the food protein. Keep the area damp during the dwell — dry mid-dwell stops the enzyme work.

homeSolutions.agent: Baking Soda + Water for Acid + Odor
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 tablespoon baking soda with 1 cup cool water. Dampen a cloth, press on the spot, let sit 5 minutes. Blot dry with a dry towel. This neutralizes remaining stomach acid — important because the acid can fade carpet dye AND the baking soda absorbs odor.
Press a thick DRY towel on the spot with a heavy book or pan on top for 30 minutes. Repeat with a fresh towel. Pulls moisture UP out of the backing before it settles into the pad.
Once the spot is damp not wet, dust baking soda generously on the area. Point a fan at it. Let dry overnight (12 hours). Vacuum off next day.

homeSolutions.agent: Smell-Check Next Day — Escalate If Odor Persists
homeSolutions.instruction: Sniff the area next morning in daylight. If any smell remains, OR a ring wicks back as the carpet dried, the pad is contaminated beyond surface treatment. Call a professional carpet cleaner. Tell them: "Vomit on [nylon / wool / synthetic] carpet, age [fresh / overnight], home-treated with Dawn + enzyme + baking soda + weighted-towel extract. [Odor persists / ring returned / area-larger-than-plate / suspected pad contamination]."

steps: 1. Honest scope: large area, wool, or pad soak = call a pro.
2. Gloves. Scrape solids onto disposable paper. Bag it.
3. Cold water + Dawn on a DAMP cloth (never pour).
4. Enzyme pretreat on a damp cloth, 15–20 min dwell.
5. Baking soda + water rinse for acid + odor.
7. Baking soda dry dust + fan overnight.
8. Smell-check next day. Odor return = batting contaminated = pro.
products: Clean white cloths + heavy dry towel + weight
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop home treatment if the vomit soaked into the pad, covers more than a dinner plate, is on wool carpet, or odor returns after drying. Call a carpet cleaner with hot-water extraction.
warnings: NEVER pool liquid on carpet — pad contamination
NEVER use hot water — sets protein + activates backing adhesive
NEVER ammonia-based cleaners — attracts pets, feeds bacteria
Pad contamination + odor = pro-only from that point

neverDo: Never hot water — cooks protein + activates backing adhesive
Never ammonia-based cleaners on carpet body-fluid stains
Never skip the scope check — pro-lane threshold is LOW
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / olefin / polyester face yarn: tolerates standard chemistry
Backing + pad: no pooled liquid, ever

materialWarnings: Pro-lane threshold is LOW on vomit-carpet
Hot wash / steam not an option here
Cat urine pattern — ammonia cleaners make it worse

escalation.when: Large spill (> dinner plate), wool carpet, pad soak suspected, or odor persists after full home cycle.
escalation.whatToTell: "Vomit on [nylon / wool / synthetic] carpet, age [fresh / overnight], home-treated with Dawn + enzyme + baking soda + weighted-towel extract. [Odor persists / ring returned / pad contamination suspected.]"
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with hot-water extraction

contextQuestions: How large is the affected area?
Did the vomit pool / soak in (liquid sitting > 1 second)?
Carpet fiber — nylon, olefin, polyester, wool?
First time on this spot, or repeat event?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Pad contamination is the invisible failure mode. Honest early escalation saves hours and avoids wasted home chemistry. Large-area or walked-on-wet = pro.
